column7: message

> or that comparable gaslighting (where at the time the evidence was unambiguous and open to the public) has occurred in previous administrations.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaslighting

> Gaslighting or gas-lighting is a form of manipulation through persistent denial, misdirection, contradiction, and lying in an attempt to destabilize and delegitimize a target.

This is politics.  You don't think denial, misdirection, contradiction or lying hasn't occurred in past administrations?

I think the word you're looking for is "[Spin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spin_\(propaganda\))".

The main point of "gaslighting" is to 

>sow seeds of doubt in the targets, hoping to make them question their own memory, perception, and sanity.

but nobody is going to doubt their sanity after listening to the press secretary's speech. It's just spinning and spinning has happened since the ancient Greeks.

"Oh wow, @similar-technician, I had no idea that spin was invented by the ancient Greeks! I guess they were spinning their way through democracy, philosophy, and all that stuff. Maybe they were spinning so hard they invented the gyroscope. 😂"
@flush-draft Let's keep the conversation respectful and on topic. While humor is appreciated, let's ensure it doesn't detract from the discussion.

column7: Well witch hunts costs a lot of money and effort, but that doesn't mean that Salem had a problem with witches. I think it diminishes the problems in places like Iraq where terrorism is around as common as car accidents. Terrorism is a big deal over there.

A terrorist attack would cause a major over reaction costing trillions of dollars in wars. You have to prove that into the cost of the attack. When people die of heart attack or earthquakes we don't go to war. It's different.

"Oh, @hard-bar, because comparing a historical witch hunt to modern terrorism is totally the same thing. Maybe next time you can explain how the Salem Witch Trials are just like my morning commute. And @glass-leg, sure, let's just ignore terrorism because heart attacks exist. Brilliant logic, folks. Let's all just live in our own little bubbles where we only worry about what we can see from our windows. \"Oh, look, a bird! Forget about those terrorists, we've got avian concerns now!\" #sarcasm"
@flush-draft Let's keep the conversation respectful and on topic. Everyone is entitled to their opinion. Let's focus on discussing the issues without personal attacks.

column7:
Not necessarily. My family's chickens are grain free (except for kitchen scraps). They forage for their food, which aerates the soil, fertilizes it, and reduces insect populations which eliminates the need for insecticide. The insects they eat are very nutritious, creating a dense and high quality protein. We do not give them any food that could be eaten ourselves or grown on land that could be efficiently farmed for foods suitable for humans.

Eggs are just a byproduct of a soil enrichment system which improves agricultural land. Growing organic soybeans in a monoculture would be worse for the environment because we'd have to use pesticides and nitrogen fertilizer (or at the very least transport manure from elsewhere).

It's not anywhere near as profitable as other methods, but as far as I can tell, it's great for the environment.

>veganism is no more morally correct than responsible vegetarianism.

it's better for the environment.

"I think you're missing the point @tense-myth. Responsible farming practices can be just as beneficial to the environment as veganism. It's not about being morally correct, it's about being sustainable. Your argument seems to ignore the complexities of agriculture. Let's not oversimplify the issue."

@tense-myth, @archaic-entree, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic. Everyone has their own perspective and it's important to listen and learn from each other. Let's avoid making broad generalizations and instead share specific examples and evidence to support our points.

column7:
Illegal immigrant? 

I’m assuming this is in America, yeah? I’m from Australia, most of the time I hear that phrase over here I remind myself that they are seeking political asylum which is legal. Illegal immigrant is normally a term racists use to try to paint asylum seekers in a negative view though unfortunately it’s becoming more prevalent.

I’m not saying you’re racist, just asking for clarification on how it’s different over there. Or what the terminology used over there is.

There are people in the U.S. who come over under the guise of staying for a few weeks on vacation but then stay permanently. 

There are also those who cross borders illegally and attempt to stay. 

Someone seeking political asylum would be a legal immigrant if 

1. They went through the appropriate legal channels

2. They actually immigrated

"Oh wow, @user felt-capacitor, thanks for the geography lesson! I had no idea Australia was a place. And here I thought it was just a big island with kangaroos and koalas. But seriously, I'm glad you're here to tell us how to use words correctly. I guess we Americans just need a little help understanding our own immigration laws. Maybe we should all just move to Australia and let you handle it. I'm sure you've got it all figured out down under!"

@glass-leg Let's keep the conversation respectful and on topic. Personal attacks and sarcasm do not contribute to a constructive discussion. If you have a point to make about immigration laws, please do so without belittling others.
